Guille Ojeda • 3rd+ Sr Innovation Architect | AWS & GenAI | Creator of Simple AWS (45k+ readers) | Speaker 3y Jonathan Hall that's not an accurate comparison (if you're taking about take-home challenges). And I think this also answers Jeroen C Commandeur 's question. Audition duration: 30m. Tom Cruise's time spent: 1h (30m reading, 30m auditioning). Casting director's time spent: 30m (total 30m if no assistants). Time to review past work: 1h. Tom Cruise's time spent: 0. Casting director's time spent: 1h (total 1h if no assistants). You can replace "audition" with "interview" and the times will be the same. It also works for reviewing past work, whether you're reviewing Tom Cruise's acting roles or software projects. Now here's where it gets interesting. Coding challenge duration: 2h (or 10 sometimes). Tom Cruise's time spent: 2h. Reviewer's time spent: 10m. So, Jonathan, auditioning is actually comparable to an interview (especially a live coding one). Which we also don't like. And Jeroen, if I as an interviewer want to minimize my time spent, it's far better for me to ask you to waste 2 hours so I can cut my review time to 10 minutes. So, yes, my time is worth a lot more than yours, you have to do the challenge. …more Like Reply

Guille Ojeda • 3rd+ Sr Innovation Architect | AWS & GenAI | Creator of Simple AWS (45k+ readers) | Speaker 3y We could argue that a real project is MUCH more representative of real work than a challenge, and would let me evaluate your chances of being a good employee a lot better than a challenge (because that's what we're evaluating, not expertise). I imagine he must be building the next leetcode or hackerrank, which is 100% a valid business and fits exactly what he said. And in that case, challenges are A LOT better for me to predict your performance as an employee than real projects, because that's literally what you'll be doing all day. If that's not the case though, well, there's a reason Tom Cruise never does code challenges. …more Like Reply

Eric Oud Ammerveld • 3rd+ Unavailable for assignments 3y Everyone has it’s ways to get an idea of a candidates competence. Jonathan Hall • 3rd+ I rescue Golang projects written by AI | Sign up to learn about Go every day boldlygo.tech/daily 3y George Hotz The problem with a challenge, even a good, realistic one like yours, is that it's still done in an artificial setting. On any healthy team, such a problem is solved in a context that simply cannot be replicated in a take home challenge. Teamwork, communication, brainstorming, considering alternatives to the problem (not just the solution). These are all things that no challenge can approximate. The things a challenge CAN approximate (technical coding ability, coding style, etc) can be very easily gleaned by looking at existing code. Insisting that someone with a proven track record needs to do YOUR exercise to re-prove that is at best wasteful, and at worst insulting. …more Like Reply

Dipender Kaushik • 3rd+ Games Designer | Developer | Founder at Dunali Games 3y I like hiring by a coding challenge. Here's what I think. Yeah you have a portfolio and what not. That's great, but what I do not know is: 1. your thought process 2. time you take to implement your thoughts 3. coding and organizing practices All candidates have different portfolios and have worked on wide range of projects. I as a company have a specific need. With a standard challenge I can compare all the candidates with single scale and that scale is customized to what I need. You maybe a great programmer but your way of working could be completely different from how my company works. Some people get really adamant when you ask them to adapt. They have certain career goals and they want to stick by it. Which is fine but I need a team that works well together. And I can spend time reading each person's detailed portfolio, old code and guess they might fit, or I could see how they did what I need them to do, in the time I need it done. Such a candidate who is willing to put in time is someone who takes initiative and is a good sign. Coz a lot of times I'll need someone to try something new, something uncomfortable & this person has a proven record with me, that they can get out of their comfort zone and attempt it, a BIG PLUS. …more Like Reply

Jeroen C Commandeur • 3rd+ Freelance Software Craftsman (edited) 3y Dipender Kaushik I'm not saying that at all. I'm saying certain solutions are not adequate enough. I'm not saying don't use a solution at all. In this case, I'm saying that if you already have -actual proof- that a candidate has certain skills acquired, a test won't add much and can even be a disadvantage because lots of people just don't perform well with tests, while they can still be excellent at the job itself. Hence, check skills, do an interview to see if there is a vibe and if so, give them a shot. You'll find out if they fit the team early on. Most of the time, I think you will be good especially if you involved your other devs in the interview. This saves everybody a lot of time and also makes sure everybody invests the right amount of time to the whole process. We are now talking in circles I think. Your position and mine are clear. Thanks for the civilized debate. :-) …more Like Reply

George Hotz George Hotz replied to Andrew Baker’s comment on this George Hotz George Hotz George Hotz 3yr • I'm shocked when people write in saying they want a job, but refuse to do our coding challenge. The job is a coding challenge! I guess it's an easy way to screen people out who'd never fit in here, every job I've applied for I'm excited when they give me coding. I love coding, it's very concrete and can be evaluated without bias. George Hotz George Hotz replied to Luka Eerens’ comment on this George Hotz George Hotz George Hotz 4yr • When I see Autonomous Vehicle companies doing IPOs, I am so saddened that they are that desperate for their bags that they scam the public out of it. TuSimple did an IPO with less revenue than comma. And now Argo is talking about it. Sad. This is not the way. AUTONOMOUS VEHICLES ARE NOT REAL. YOU ARE A SEED STAGE STARTUP WITH A TECH DEMO. YOU DO NOT EVEN HAVE PRODUCT MARKET FIT, YOU SHOULDN'T BE DOING A FUCKING IPO. Seriously, how did anyone read the financial numbers in this S-1 and invest in this company? In 2020, 1.8 million revenue, 5.2 million cost of revenue, and 132 million in R&D. https://lnkd.in/d8pWEmb …more 73 comments 12 reposts Like Comment Repost Send Luka Eerens • 3rd+ Head of Digital Transformation @ Vardags | Ex-Conjecture, Ex-Planting Space | Carnegie Mellon Robotics (MRSD '18) 4y This is a clear problem. The most common questions I got when pitching my projects was how are we different. Yet when I asked this same question to heads of autonomous driving companies giving talks at our university, they would come up with a meandering diatribe equivalent to "we're all doing the same thing, but we have a better team", and every director gave this kind of answer. Don't they get asked these questions during pitches? How are these kinds of answers satisfactory? Where is the scrutiny? Bizarrely some of these companies have seemed to evade scrutiny from the Seed Round all the way to IPO. Creating this precedence of over-promising while underdelivering really salts the fields for the entire industry. …more Like Reply 5 replies 5 Replies on Luka Eerens’ comment George Hotz Author 4y I think the issue is that the investors don't fundamentally care about produced value. An IPO is a win for them, so the question isn't "will this team succeed" but rather "will this team convince enough others they might succeed that we can exit with a good multiple" …more Like Reply Marcus Kolade • 3rd+ Cybersecurity Engineer 4y George Hotz True, it's also sad what the markets have come to. Like Reply Rob S. • 3rd+ Technical Program Management | Physical AI, Robotics & Engineering Development | Fleet Production, Validation & Scaling | Semiconductor NPI | Product Management | Ex Zoox, VW/Audi 4y Juicero was my favorite. Totally agree incentives are not aligned, in general. I would also add that investors I met made their money or experience in internet 1.0 or something not automotive, so there is no experienced investment base for autonomous. I think that is/will change, but agree with the other comments here. …more Like Reply Collapse replies Load more comments

There are very talented engineers putting in their best efforts at these startups to make sure when it happens, it happens right. And I believe the investors already decided based on many factors when they fund, as they are the real foreseers and risk takers. While anyone here can make such blunt opinions on the social media, who might later regret not investing in the same when the opportunity was made available using the IPO, one good example is Tesla’s stocks as of today. …more Like Reply 5 replies 5 Replies on Hashim A.’s comment George Hotz Author (edited) 4y Hahahaha okay the developers get an A for effort and an F for having accomplished anything useful. The whole hand coded LIDAR + HD map approach is total junk. "It happens right" is a bad excuse. I've heard the stupidest stories from inside these companies (ask about Uber's bike rack issue). Waymo is the furthest along this path and it's pretty public what they have. It sort of works using really expensive cars in a small area in a flat suburb. It isn't really starting to work better. There's a reason everyone is leaving that company and they now have "co-CEOs". No good company ever had "co-CEOs" The Roadster had shipped like 1,000 units when Tesla IPOed in 2010. I drove in one in 2008. Sounds like revenue and product market fit (Tesla had 93M in revenue in 2009 on 85M cost of revenue). Very unlike the SDC scam. Tesla does SDCs too, the difference is they make money off them providing value to customers instead of investment scams. …more Like Reply Hashim A. • 3rd+ | M | Go Blue ! 4y George Hotz Developers do get an A for sure, but companies are built and scaled up by those same A’s without which companies wouldn’t survive or even be talking about IPOs. Excuse is still valid, as there will be life’s at stake when people want to use such and such on-road and to put their trust in. At least I do not want to be in a car with faulty behavior identified by those ace engineers already to be killing me or someone at all. Hence the wait game by many . And whoever is already there in the market with their publicly available information is just a minute part of what’s achieved as a company, whichever you want to name.. their secret sauce is never revealed. Hence there are measures taken by NDAs or such. Waymo is the furthest along this path and it's pretty public what they have. It sort of works using really expensive cars in a small area in a flat suburb. It isn't really starting to work better. There's a reason everyone is leaving that company and they now have "co-CEOs". No good company ever had "co-CEOs" The Roadster had shipped like 1,000 units when Tesla IPOed in 2010. I drove in one in 2008. Sounds like revenue and product market fit (Tesla had 93M in revenue in 2009 on 85M cost of revenue). Very unlike the SDC scam. Tesla does SDCs too, the difference is they make money off them providing value to customers instead of investment scams. …more Like Reply Hashim A. • 3rd+ | M | Go Blue ! 4y George Hotz Developers do get an A for sure, but companies are built and scaled up by those same A’s without which companies wouldn’t survive or even be talking about IPOs. I'm not totally sure I buy the "decentralized" tagline Like Reply Mark Wilcox • 3rd+ Owner Operator at 21e8 4y ETH marketing is very much singularity / decentralisation theatre. But because investors have made a big USD ROI, they don’t worry about the fact it didn’t deliver in reality. So it’s sort of just a neo Google or FB (albeit much more efficient). This is mostly because it works through investments and derivatives, not through cash transactions. We need proper information supply chains and pricing mechanisms for data, rather than engineered incentive systems where people spend all their time and energy on made up things …more Like Reply George Hotz Author (edited) 4y Mark Wilcox I certainly agree that's where it's at now. But DeFi is a game for sharks. At the end of the forty differently shaped Ponzi and gambling apps (u like ICO, u play FOMO3D, wha bout yield farm?), the real things should be what are left holding the value I think. There's competition in money supply and no bailouts at the end of the day; that's the difference here. Eventually, with a real money supply and a long enough timeline, the Ponzi speculators will go broke. Some will cash out their USD ROI, but if crypto wins... Stablecoin. Now that'll be a funny meme in the future. …more Like Reply Mark Wilcox • 3rd+ Owner Operator at 21e8 4y I think it is propped up by PoW and will look quite different when (if?) they move to PoS and those GPUs go elsewhere Like Reply George Hotz Author (edited) 4y Maybe. I used to be very bearish on PoS, but I've come around a bit. Let's say I haven't locked anything in the ETH2 contract though. I think there's a lot of demand for the "Ethereum Computer" product. But DeFi is a game for sharks. There's competition in money supply and no bailouts at the end of the day; that's the difference here. Eventually, with a real money supply and a long enough timeline, the Ponzi speculators will go broke. Some will cash out their USD ROI, but if crypto wins... Stablecoin. George Hotz George Hotz replied to Michael Cooley’s comment on this George Hotz George Hotz George Hotz 5yr • This is a personal post. Do you find the language around "human resources" dehumanizing? Recruiters trying to pawn off engineers by the dozen. Outsourcing middle men discussing cost reduction of "the labor side." Binders full of engineers. Top pedigree engineers like you are pitching a hot new racehorse. I'm not a fucking resource. I'm a person. Stand up to the professional managerial class and don't tolerate this language. …more 42 comments 16 reposts Like Comment Repost Send Michael Cooley • 3rd+ Stygian Industries (edited) 4y George Hotz this is why i admire you so much. on face you are 1. arrogant 2. rude at times and 3. (to be honest) snobby. I say arrogant because because you charge 1000 dollars for a 30 minute phone call. pour I say rude at times because you read or at least open messages from people and do not reply. no hi bye fuck off or otherwise I say snobby because you look down your nose at people of average or less intelligence. Im not against your snobbyness because its directed at the willfuly stupid and corrupt cons of the tech industry lol This comment shows you are not a mean person. You are human and can be compassionate …more Like Reply 8 replies 8 Replies on Michael Cooley’s comment See previous replies George Hotz Author (edited) 4y Most people are wasting your time on the phone. All worthwhile things can be said just as well over e-mail. The phone is only because they want to give you a sales pitch, hence the $1000. What's your expected ROI? Most people are wasting your time in messages, hence no reply. LinkedIn messages are the biggest trash heap. There's certainly ways to get me to read your message, LinkedIn isn't one of them. And if it's dumb, I still will ignore it. Intelligence is a big predictor of whether people will accomplish interesting things. Not the only predictor, but a very good one. This isn't about mean or not mean. It's about talented engineers tolerating this crappy dehumanizing treatment from "management" when they really shouldn't. PS: Couldn't care less if I "inspire people", and I sure as hell won't change my behavior because of it. Though I do like being entertaining :) …more Like Reply Peter Novak • 3rd+ Product owner and designer at IXPERTA 4y George Hotz Rofl, this is one of the most arrogant and idiotic comments I have read in the past few months and I have been reading the newsfeed of our prime minister, so the competition was pretty tough. Phone obviously works better for a lot of things, especially since there are cues that are unavailable in email, and the communication is synchronous unlike email. Read studies on it. They are publicly available, try fucking google scholar, you will look smarter in a discussion next time. Rubbish about expected ROI? Oh my god, seriously? You just copied stuff from your profile. Copying yourself is a solid sign of either starting to be elderly and demented or just completely lacking creativity. …more Like Reply Peter Novak • 3rd+ Product owner and designer at IXPERTA 4y George Hotz Comment about messages - another load of rubbish. Obviously you did not understand shit from what OP was talking about. He was talking about politeness, respect and humility. Apparently these mean absolutely nothing to you. No matter what the people write to you, it matters to them - so if you had the heart you were presenting in your post, you would understand. But you are just a jerk who is full of himself. Intelligence is obviously a great predictor, but definitely not the only one. And if anything your post shows both lack of understanding of written text and empathy. Which are apparently predicted by intelligence leading me to believe your scores are fascinatingly low considering your impact. Yes. And it is also about tolerating your arrogant imbecility, when we really should not. So I do not. P.S.: But you should care. Being a high profile person is about considering what do you represent and what that does to the world. Your post represented some goodness and goodwill. This load of shit represents nothing but an arrogant son of a bitch, who believes he is better than others. P.P.S.: You are an idiot. …more Like Reply Peter Novak • 3rd+ Product owner and designer at IXPERTA 4y George Hotz I seriously wonder... were you fucking drunk while you were writing this? Did you take a hit in the head? Seriously most of your posts are pretty good, but the amount of genuine, absolute, obnoxious, moronic and totally marvelous bullshit is utterly breathtaking. …more Like Reply Peter Novak • 3rd+ Product owner and designer at IXPERTA 4y George Hotz But to reply to your imbecilic moronic comment, which you copied from yourself - but in the profile it was at least, as you say, entertaining, while now it is just cheap and stupid - my ROI is WAY over 1k dollars per hour. But for most people I do not charge. I reply politely. And I treat everyone as equal, no matter their intelligence or social status. And so, I take time to reply if I read something, even if it was sorry, not interested. And this helps produce a better society. Because not everything is about money, efficiency and ROI. There are things that are much more valuable. But I doubt you would understand. Also, when I see someone behaving like a spoiled piece of shit, I point it out. Very harshly. No matter the recipients social status, intelligence or power. Just like I did now. Seriously, long time since I have seen such a nice heartfelt comment, trying to help and with deep humility admire someone (like the guy did you) followed by such blatant arrogance, ignorance and breaking every possible social norm available. Go read some fucking books about emotional intelligence, once you are done with reading about communication modalities. Seriously, long time since I have seen such a nice heartfelt comment, trying to help and with deep humility admire someone (like the guy did you) followed by such blatant arrogance, ignorance and breaking every possible social norm available. Go read some fucking books about emotional intelligence, once you are done with reading about communication modalities. Apparently communication is not your strong point so educate yourself …more Like Reply

George Hotz George Hotz replied to Rory Garton-Smith’s comment on this George Hotz George Hotz George Hotz 5yr • This is a personal post. Do you find the language around "human resources" dehumanizing? Recruiters trying to pawn off engineers by the dozen. Outsourcing middle men discussing cost reduction of "the labor side." Binders full of engineers. Top pedigree engineers like you are pitching a hot new racehorse. I'm not a fucking resource. I'm a person. Stand up to the professional managerial class and don't tolerate this language. …more 42 comments 16 reposts Like Comment Repost Send Rory Garton-Smith • 3rd+ Co-Founder at Checkmate 5y The homogenization of diverse subsets of people into distinct 'brackets' is an age old practice from leadership dating back to the middle ages. Makes it much easier to run corporations/countries etc. Check out "Seeing Like a State" by James C Scott. You can frame it from a moral perspective "don't classify me as a resource - I'm a human" - but that's extremely out of touch with the world around you, it's not realistic to suggest that leadership with any great number of people beneath them can be in touch on a personal level with every single engineer and understand all of their unique skills in order to stop categorizing them into less wide brackets. TLDR: Respectfully, your post is idealistic and not founded in reality. …more Like Reply 1 reply 1 Comment on Rory Garton-Smith’s comment George Hotz Author (edited) 5y Love that book. Some people working at subsistence levels might not have a choice. But to those that do have a choice, stop tolerating this! And I mostly just mean the language, I understand scale, I'm not talking about being on a personal level with people. I'm talking about pushing back if anyone refers to you or others with this dehumanizing language. …more Like Reply Load more comments

George Hotz George Hotz replied to their own comment on this Vladislav Voroninski Vladislav Voroninski • 3rd+ Premium • 3rd+ CEO at Helm.ai (We're hiring!) CEO at Helm.ai (We're hiring!) 5yr • Follow After three years of operating in stealth mode, Helm.ai is excited to announce a breakthrough in computer vision and autonomous driving technology called Deep Teaching. As a first demonstration, we trained a neural network for lane detection without any human annotation or simulation on ten million images from dashcam footage from across the world, resulting in robustness to a variety of corner cases out of the box. We placed #1 in the CVPR lane detection benchmark on all metrics using this neural network with minimal engineering effort. We then built an autonomous steering system using just one camera and one GPU, which is able to drive on steep and curvy mountain roads without any maps, Lidar or GPS, nor without being trained on any data from these roads, performing well beyond today's production systems across a wide range of conditions. To the best of our knowledge, Deep Teaching is the first method to train a semantic segmentation neural network in a fully unsupervised fashion which surpasses the computer vision state of the art, and has applications well beyond autonomous driving. More announcements coming soon! hashtag #autonomousdriving hashtag #deeplearning hashtag #ai hashtag #artificialintelligence hashtag #computervision https://lnkd.in/gNVAHsf …more Helm.ai Autonomous Steering Benchmark youtube.com 75 comments 12 reposts Like Comment Repost Send George Hotz (edited) 5y If you'd like something like this in your car today, check out https://comma.ai/ Runs an open source driving agent, https://github.com/commaai/openpilot with one camera and one GPU. No maps, LIDAR or GPS either. Supports comma two hardware, https://shop.comma.ai/. $1200 will have this up on the car you probably already own. Or build it yourself for cheaper, the software is free and runs on PCs with webcams. https://medium.com/@comma_ai/self-driving-car-for-free-82e871fe0587 …more Like Reply 6 replies 6 Replies on George Hotz’s comment See previous replies Vladislav Voroninski Author CEO at Helm.ai (We're hiring!) 5y George Hotz We only disagree about what the bottleneck is. Functional safety is important and tractable. Human-level perception, which governs safety at the tail end is still an open problem. If you have doubts about what's hard about this stretch of page mill, go check it out! …more Like Reply Load more comments

Lucky Munro • 3rd+ Red Team Leader - Cybersecurity Senior Specialist at Motional 6y Calvin S. Actually an immense amount of code would be required to make it work properly if the cars drive themselves, which I believe to be the implied intent of your comment. The object of a demolition derby is to destroy your opponents while not getting destroyed yourself. The car would need to: drive in both directions, have object collision avoidance of moving objects, real-time mapping from lidar/radar/camera, and target analysis to determine the most vulnerable point of said moving object. On top of the normal controls necessary to make it work...Maybe instead just give them to some rednecks and let me go to town. Required code: zero. …more Like Reply
